title: Measuring Semantic Drift
concept: Semantic drift occurs when the distribution of user queries changes over time, causing the model's performance to degrade compared to offline testing.

exercises.prompt: What happens to the language and topics that human users search for over time?

options.text: It stays exactly the same forever.
It stops changing once computers are turned off.

exercises.explain: Just like fashion or slang, the way people talk and search online changes constantly. This means our AI models must adapt too.

cards.title: Why Does AI Get Outdated?
cards.body: Imagine building an AI that knows everything about fashion. It works perfectly in January. But by July, users are asking for 'cottagecore' and 'crocs'—terms your AI has never learned. Why does AI get worse even when we don't change its code?

cards.title: What is Semantic Drift?
cards.body: Semantic drift happens when the meaning or types of queries your users type change over time. Because the real world changes, the language users use shifts away from the static data you used to train the model, making the AI less accurate.

cards.title: The Seasonal Toy Store
cards.body: Imagine you run a toy store. During winter, everyone asks for 'sleds' and 'coats'. When summer arrives, customers suddenly start asking for 'swimsuits' and 'beach balls'. Your winter-themed storefront is no longer helpful because the customer's language and needs have drifted with the season!

cards.title: The 'Corona' Shift
cards.body: Before 2020, if someone searched for 'corona', they were probably looking for a beer brand or the outer atmosphere of the sun. After 2020, the word almost exclusively referred to the coronavirus. An AI trained in 2019 would fail to understand this new meaning.

cards.title: Myth: Perfect Models Stay Perfect
cards.body: People think that once an AI model passes offline tests with 99% accuracy, it will stay that way forever. In reality, language is alive. Without monitoring and updating, real-world accuracy drifts downward as human behavior changes.

cards.title: The Gap Widens
cards.body: Semantic drift happens because our training data is frozen in time, but user behavior is always moving forward.

right.text: Constantly evolving (e.g., today's slang and news)

exercises.prompt: What is the primary cause of semantic drift in AI systems?

options.text: Real-world user queries and language change over time.
The model's database files get corrupted on the server.
Computers get slower at running deep learning models.
The AI is actively trying to rewrite its own code.

exercises.explain: Semantic drift is driven by changes in user behavior and real-world language, which makes the older training data less representative of current queries.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a model passes its offline tests perfectly, it is guaranteed to never experience semantic drift in production.
exercises.explain: Offline tests only use historical data. They cannot predict how language and user trends will change in the future.

exercises.prompt: Complete the sentence to describe why AI performance drops over time.
exercises.sentence: When user search topics change over time, the AI experiences semantic [blank].
exercises.explain: Semantic drift describes how the distribution of user queries changes over time, causing the AI's real-world performance to degrade.

exercises.prompt: Identify the scenario that describes semantic drift:

options.text: An AI chatbot starts throwing database connection errors during peak hours.
A food delivery AI fails because users start using the word 'spicy' to mean 'exciting' rather than 'hot'.
A developer updates the model's neural network architecture to make it run faster.

exercises.explain: The sentence about the restaurant app represents semantic drift because the meaning of 'spicy' changed for users over time, causing the model to become inaccurate.

exercises.prompt: Your team notices that your AI search engine is getting worse at finding relevant answers compared to when you first launched it. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: A bug in the model's code that causes it to run slower over time.
A shift in user queries and language over time that makes the model less accurate.
A technique used to compress the size of neural networks.
When the model starts generating random programming errors.

exercises.explain: Semantic drift occurs when users start using new terms or shifting the meaning of their queries over time, making older training data outdated and causing model performance to drop.
